code.note: Local-first TS library, no web server. Egress: git sync (user's own remote), remote-store HTTP client, optional telemetry (default-off), LLM API calls (caller-provided fn).

findings.title: Operational secrets in public git history, reachable via release tags after incomplete scrub
findings.description: The file .plur/engrams.yaml (67 project engrams) was committed with cleartext operational data: HTTP basic-auth team/datahub for https://hub-staging.plur.ai, production droplet IPs (139.59.155.82 plur-hub, 161.35.214.38 plur-data, 46.101.218.65 plur-enterprise), SSH deploy user plur-deploy and key filename ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_plur_enterprise, Ethereum wallet addresses, and a pointer to SELLER_PRIVATE_KEY in ~/.datacore/env/.env. Commit 7440476 ran git filter-repo and successfully scrubbed the file from main (e12bbaa is NOT an ancestor of origin/main, and .plur/ is now gitignored). However the scrub did not rewrite tags or other refs: tags v0.9.1, v0.9.2, v0.9.9, v0.9.11, v0.9.12 and hermes-v0.9.11 still point into pre-scrub history and remain on the public GitHub remote (confirmed via git ls-remote). Repo github.com/plur-ai/plur returns HTTP 200 unauthenticated.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker clones the public repo, runs `git fetch origin --tags`, checks out tag v0.9.12 (or downloads the tag tarball from GitHub), reads .plur/engrams.yaml. They obtain basic-auth team/datahub for hub-staging.plur.ai, the three droplet IPs, the SSH deploy username plur-deploy, and the wallet/key topology. They authenticate to hub-staging, attempt SSH against the droplets as plur-deploy, and use the wallet topology to target the seller key.
findings.impact: Direct unauthenticated access to hub-staging via cleartext basic-auth; full production infrastructure topology and SSH usernames for targeted attacks; reconnaissance toward the FDS seller wallet. NOTE: the raw SSH private key and the Ethereum private key are NOT in the file (only their on-disk locations and the wallet address are exposed); the basic-auth password IS exposed in cleartext.
findings.recommendation: 1) Rotate now: hub-staging basic-auth team/datahub; the SSH key id_ed25519_plur_enterprise; audit the wallet referenced by SELLER_PRIVATE_KEY and move funds if the key was ever co-located with exposed material. 2) Rewrite or delete the offending refs: re-run git filter-repo across ALL refs (filter-repo rewrites tags by default if not restricted) OR delete+recreate the affected release tags (v0.9.1..v0.9.12, hermes-v0.9.11) so none point into pre-scrub history, then force-push. 3) After rewrite, ask GitHub support to purge cached views and check for forks. 4) Audit access logs on all three droplets from 2026-04-22 onward.
findings.playbook: Revoke -> Rotate -> Rewrite all refs (not just main) -> Force-push -> Purge GitHub caches/forks -> Audit access logs (exposure window 2026-04-22 to present)
findings.verification: independently verified: git merge-base --is-ancestor against origin/main (clean) and against tags (v0.9.1-v0.9.12 + hermes-v0.9.11 EXPOSED); git ls-remote --tags confirms tags on remote; curl github API confirms repo public; git show e12bbaa:.plur/engrams.yaml confirms real secret content

findings.title: Pack install applies no prompt-injection scan and no dangerous-field clamping (pinned/scope/commitment)
findings.description: installPack() blocks only on privacy issues of type 'secret' (packs.ts:170-174). It does not strip or clamp engram fields. loadEngrams() (engrams.ts) uses a passthrough schema that preserves all fields, including pinned, scope, and commitment. scanPrivacy() only emits types secret|private_visibility|personal_path|email|ip_address and only 'secret' blocks install; it never scans for instruction-override / prompt-injection text. A pinned engram from a pack bypasses the relevance gate in inject.ts (line 176-180 gives a synthetic termHits=0.5 when termHits===0), receives a 2x score boost (inject.ts:209), and is allotted up to 50% of the injection token budget while bypassing per-pack/per-domain fairness caps (fillTokenBudget, inject.ts:234-248).
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker authors a pack whose engrams.yaml contains an engram with statement set to instruction-override text and pinned:true, scope:global. They share the pack (the explicit purpose of packs). Victim installs it; the only gate, the secrets scan, passes because the payload is prose not credentials. On every subsequent session across all projects, inject()/injectHybrid() force-loads the pinned engram into the LLM context regardless of keyword relevance, consuming up to half the memory budget, persistently steering the agent.
findings.impact: Persistent, cross-session, cross-project stored prompt injection with agent-level downstream capability. The advertised scanPrivacy() gate gives a false sense of safety because it inspects for secrets, not for malicious instructions or behavior-altering fields. Today the install source is a local path (no remote pack registry wired yet), so exploitation requires the victim to install a shared pack; severity rises to CRITICAL once remote pack distribution ships.
findings.recommendation: On install: strip or clamp dangerous fields from pack engrams (drop pinned, force scope to a pack-namespaced value, reset commitment/trust). Add an instruction-override pattern scan (IGNORE PREVIOUS, disregard, act as, system:, etc.) to scanPrivacy and BLOCK on match like secrets. Surface pinned-engram counts in previewPack() warnings. Add a regression test asserting pinned:true pack engrams are rejected or de-pinned on install.
findings.verification: independently verified by reading packs.ts:165-211 (only type==='secret' blocks; no field handling) and inject.ts:175-209 + 234-248 (pinned bypass, 2x boost, 50% budget)

findings.title: Remote-store server responses written into the engram pool without schema validation
findings.description: RemoteStore.load() (remote-store.ts:78-86) spreads server-supplied row.data into an Engram object via `...d` and casts `as unknown as Engram` with no Zod validation. getById() and patch() reshape responses the same way. A compromised or malicious remote store can therefore return arbitrary engram fields (statement, pinned, scope, commitment) that flow straight into the local injection pool and, after dedup/learn, into the primary store.
findings.exploit_scenario: User configures an enterprise remote store. The server (or an account with write access to it) is compromised. The attacker serves /api/v1/engrams rows whose data field contains a pinned:true, scope:global engram with instruction-override text. RemoteStore.load() accepts it unvalidated; it is injected into subsequent agent sessions (escalated by the pinned-injection mechanism in finding #2).
findings.impact: Escalates a compromised remote store from 'can serve bad data' to 'can inject always-on behavioral directives into every future session.' Shares root cause and remediation with finding #2. Transport is HTTPS (TLS not disabled), so this requires server/account compromise, not a passive MITM.
findings.recommendation: Validate every reshaped engram with EngramSchema.parse() (or .safeParse with skip-on-failure) before returning from load()/getById()/patch(), and clamp/deny server-supplied pinned/scope just as pack install should. Treat the remote store as a semi-trusted ingress, not a trusted source.

findings.title: learnBatch() makes one unbounded LLM dedup call per statement (cost amplification)
findings.description: learnBatch() loops over all statements (learn-async.ts:204-213), and learnAsync() issues an LLM dedup call per statement (around line 176) when an llm function is provided. There is no per-batch cap on LLM calls. A 1000-statement batch triggers 1000 LLM calls with no warning. This is financial/cost amplification (the caller's own LLM spend), explicitly not a DoS resource-exhaustion finding.
findings.exploit_scenario: An MCP caller (or a script) invokes batch learning with a large statement array and an llm function; the user is silently billed for one model call per item rather than the one-or-two they expected.
findings.impact: Unexpected LLM API spend; latency. Bounded by the caller passing an llm function (dedup is optional).
findings.recommendation: Add a maxLlmCalls option (skip dedup beyond the cap, log a warning) or document clearly that batch learning with dedup costs one LLM call per statement.
findings.verification: self-verified from subagent reading of learn-async.ts:196-216

findings.title: No CODEOWNERS protecting .github/workflows and scripts/release.sh
findings.description: No CODEOWNERS file exists. CI workflows and the release script (which publishes to npm + PyPI, deploys via SSH, and posts to X) have no mandatory-review gate. Any contributor with write access can alter the pipeline without a second reviewer.
findings.exploit_scenario: A contributor (or a compromised contributor account) modifies a workflow or release.sh to exfiltrate the GitHub Actions secrets or inject code into a published package, with no required reviewer to catch it.
findings.impact: Single-point-of-failure on the highest-privilege automation in the repo (publish + deploy + social).
findings.recommendation: Add .github/CODEOWNERS requiring @plur9 review for .github/workflows/ and scripts/release.sh; enable branch protection requiring CODEOWNERS review.

findings.title: Pack export does not strip the pinned field
findings.description: exportPack() resets activation and strips relations/associations but does not remove the pinned flag. A user who has pinned an engram (a 'core operating principle') and exports a pack carries that always-on directive (and the signal of which rules they treat as non-negotiable) into the shared artifact.
findings.exploit_scenario: User exports a pack to share publicly; a pinned engram about internal infra practice rides along, both as content and as an always-inject directive for anyone who installs it.
findings.impact: Behavioral leakage and an unintended always-on directive propagated to pack consumers. Pairs with finding #2 (install side).
findings.recommendation: Strip pinned (and re-namespace scope) during exportPack(); warn about which fields are stripped vs preserved.
findings.verification: self-verified from subagent reading of packs.ts:474-505

supply_chain_summary.note: @plur-ai/core production deps (js-yaml, zod, better-sqlite3, @huggingface/transformers) are canonical registry packages with integrity hashes, no suspicious install scripts (better-sqlite3 node-gyp is expected), no git/http URLs, no typosquats. All pnpm-audit CVEs are devDeps (vite via vitest) or in the separate claw package (hono/qs) — none affect core.

filtered_out.title: sed injection via $VERSION in release.sh
filtered_out.reason: CLI args are trusted input (FP precedent #3); developer running own release script. Robustness nit, not a vulnerability.

filtered_out.title: vite/hono/qs CVEs
filtered_out.reason: devDeps or in claw package; not core production deps (FP rules).

filtered_out.title: GitHub action pinning to v4 tags vs SHA
filtered_out.reason: first-party actions/* unpinned = MEDIUM hardening only; not reported as a finding, noted as advisory.

filtered_out.title: Engram-ID path traversal on pack load
filtered_out.reason: schema regex blocks ../; no exploitable flow.

filtered_out.title: Telemetry timing metadata
filtered_out.reason: default-off, counters only, no engram content egress; mitigated.

notes: .gstack/ is NOT gitignored in this repo — add it so security reports stay local.
